subtitle: Side stitch and abdominal strain

strava_risk_triggers.risk_note: Side stitch most common in early training weeks and when pace increases

mild.description: Side stitch during faster efforts, eases when slowing down
mild.can_run_note: Yes — slow down, breathe deeply, press on the stitch
why: Diaphragm cramping from shallow breathing under exertion
Running too soon after eating — full stomach pulling on diaphragm ligaments
Weak deep core forcing shallow breathing pattern
relief: Slow to walk pace and press fingers firmly into the stitch
Exhale forcefully when the foot on the affected side strikes the ground
Deep belly breathing — breathe in for 3 steps, out for 2
Stretch arms overhead while walking to open the diaphragm
prevention: Do not eat within 2 hours of a hard run
Practise diaphragmatic breathing during easy runs
Build running volume gradually — stitches reduce as fitness improves
Stay well hydrated — dehydration increases stitch frequency

moderate.description: Persistent side pain or abdominal strain that does not ease mid-run
moderate.can_run_note: Walk until pain resolves — do not push through abdominal pain
why: Oblique or rectus abdominis strain from coughing, sneezing, or sudden effort
Core fatigue causing compensatory breathing patterns
Iliopsoas tightness referring into the lower abdomen
relief: Rest 2–3 days from running if muscle strain suspected
Heat on abdominal muscles if strained
prevention: Core endurance work — plank and dead bug 3× per week
Breathing rhythm practice during easy runs
Gradual introduction of tempo and interval work
Hip flexor stretching to reduce iliopsoas tension

severe.description: Sharp abdominal pain at rest, guarding, pain unrelated to exercise
severe.can_run_note: Do not run — seek medical assessment
why: Possible hernia, especially with sharp localised pain and bulge
Non-musculoskeletal cause that requires medical diagnosis
relief: GP or emergency assessment if pain is severe
Do not push through or self-treat sharp abdominal pain
prevention: Progressive core loading before returning to running
Hernia screening if recurrent groin or lower abdominal pain

see_physio_if: Abdominal pain at rest unrelated to exercise
Visible bulge in the groin or abdomen
Pain that does not resolve within 48 hours of rest
Nausea or vomiting alongside abdominal pain
